chunk_summary: This section discusses sodium targets, diuretic monitoring, and fluid restriction in pediatric chronic liver disease with ascites, focusing on hyponatremia management and evidence-based guidelines for care.
learning_objectives: Understand sodium targets and their role in managing ascites in pediatric chronic liver disease.
Learn the principles of diuretic monitoring in children with liver disease and ascites.
Identify when fluid restriction is necessary in cases of hyponatremia.
Apply evidence-based guidelines for managing ascites in pediatric patients.
key_facts: Sodium intake should be carefully managed in pediatric patients with ascites, with targets often set below 2 g/day depending on severity.
Diuretic therapy, including spironolactone and furosemide, requires close monitoring to prevent electrolyte imbalances and renal dysfunction.
Hyponatremia (serum sodium <130 mmol/L) may necessitate fluid restriction to 1,000–1,200 mL/day in older children or proportionally less in younger children.
The AASLD and EASL guidelines emphasize individualized care based on the severity of ascites and associated complications.
Nutrition support, including adequate protein intake, is critical to prevent malnutrition in children with chronic liver disease.
reference_quote: ‘Hyponatremia in pediatric patients with chronic liver disease should be managed with fluid restriction and careful monitoring of sodium levels.’ (Source: AASLD practice guidance on ascites, spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, and hepatorenal syndrome)
document_key_topics: Nutritional support in chronic liver disease
